title: Coherence-Enhanced Quantum Battery Charging with Ergotropy Stabilization
authors: Fan Yang, Hui Wang, Yusef Maleki, William J. Munro, Girish S. Agarwal, Marlan O. Scully
abstract: Quantum batteries utilize nonclassical resources to achieve charging speed and energy storage performances that surpass classical thermodynamic limits. However, the practical realization of quantum batteries is often constrained by the inevitable environment-induced dissipation of both stored ergotropy and coherence. To actively counteract these losses, we propose a dual-channel coherence framework that exploits dark-state protection to stabilize ergotropy. We conduct, for the first time, an investigation of the synergistic interplay between internal charger coherence and reservoir squeezing, the latter acting as a source of external coherence. In the resource-efficient regime where charger and battery sizes are comparable, our study shows that internal charger coherence and reservoir squeezing jointly enhance the transient charging power. Crucially, initial charger coherence is the fundamental resource for maximizing and stabilizing steady-state ergotropy through dark-state protection. Our analysis reveals that these advantages are driven by the buildup of local battery coherence, which emerges from the integration of both internal and external coherence sources. These results offer a robust pathway for high-power, stabilized energy storage in quantum architectures.
summary: This paper proposes a method to enhance and stabilize the energy storage capacity of quantum batteries by utilizing both initial charger coherence and reservoir squeezing. It identifies a synergy between these two coherence sources that boosts charging power and uses dark states to protect extractable work from environmental decay. The findings suggest a practical pathway for high-power, stable energy storage in quantum architectures.
main_problem: How to overcome the dissipation of ergotropy and coherence in quantum batteries caused by environmental coupling, while simultaneously accelerating charging power.
main_result: The study demonstrates that combining internal charger coherence with external reservoir squeezing synergistically enhances transient charging power and that initial charger coherence enables steady-state ergotropy stabilization via dark-state protection.
method: Analytical and numerical analysis using a Lindblad master equation, Dicke basis expansion, and biorthogonal formalism to examine ergotropy decomposition and time-resolved dynamics.
model_or_system: A quantum battery and charger system composed of spin ensembles (Dicke sectors) interacting via a shared squeezed vacuum reservoir.
key_observables: Total ergotropy (split into population-driven and coherence-driven components), battery energy, charging power, local battery coherence, and logarithmic negativity.
important_parameters: Charger polar angle (theta), reservoir squeezing parameter (r), relative phase (delta), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The evolution is cyclic; the reservoir is modeled using the Born-Markov approximation; the study focuses on the regime where charger and battery sizes are comparable.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows ergotropy dependence on charger angle and relative phase; Figure 3 shows scaling of energy and ergotropy; Figure 5 illustrates the time evolution of ergotropy components and coherence under different squeezing protocols.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dissipation in quantum batteries, defines the dual-channel coherence framework, analyzes the single-spin and multi-spin dynamics, explores the synergy between internal and external coherence, and proposes a resource-efficient finite-time squeezing protocol.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism for stabilizing quantum resources using reservoir engineering and dark-state protection, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum thermodynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the model. A charger (NC spins) and a battery (NB spins) are coupled to a common reservoir. The charger is prepared in a product state |θ, ϕ⟩⊗NC 1/2 , and the battery is initialized in the fully down-polarized state | ↓⟩⊗NB. The two large spheres represent the collective-spin states of the charger and battery, and the red and green arrows inside them indicate the corresponding collective spin orientations.

Figure 2: Steady-state battery performance in multi-spin systems for NB = 4 and various charger sizes: NC = 4 (blue) and NC = 8 (brown). The squeezed vacuum parameter is fixed at r = 0.5. Solid, dotted, and dashed curves represent total ergotropy WB, coherent ergotropy WC B , and population-driven ergotropy WP B, respectively. (a): Dependence on the charger polar angle θ ∈[0, π] with the relative phase fixed at δ = 0. (b): Dependence on the relative phase δ ∈[−π, π] with the charger polar angle fixed at θ = π/3.

Figure 3: Scaling of steady-state battery performance with system size N, under a fixed spin ratio NC = NB = N. Normalized battery energy EB (red and yellow) and ergotropy WB (blue and green) as functions of NB for vacuum (solid) and squeezed-vacuum (r = 0.5, dashed) reservoirs. Specifically, red (EB) and blue (WB) curves correspond to the all-spin-up charger (θ = 0), while yellow (EB) and green (WB) curves denote the spin-coherent charger (θ = π/3). All results are evaluated at the relative phase δ = 0.

Figure 4: Steady-state ergotropy components and subsystem entanglement as functions of the charger polar angle θ. Total ergotropy WB (blue solid), population contribution WP B (blue dashed) and coherence-induced contribution WC B (blue dotted) are contrasted against the battery-charger entanglement SB (red solid). Results are calculated for a squeezed-vacuum (r = 0.5) reservoir with spin numbers NB = NC = 4 at the relative phase δ = 0.

Figure 5: Time evolution of the coherent ergotropy WC B (top panel, blue), incoherent ergotropy WP B (bottom panel, blue), and the local coherence CB (top panel, orange) under different reservoir and initial state conditions: vacuum reservoir with all- spin-up charger (θ = 0) (dotted), squeezed-vacuum (r = 0.5) reservoir with all-spin-up charger (θ = 0) (dashed), vacuum reservoir with spin-coherent charger (θ = π/3) (dot-dashed), and squeezed-vacuum (r = 0.5) reservoir with spin-coherent charger (θ = π/3) (solid). Other parameters used are NC = NB = 4 and δ = 0.
